subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>BeO</mi></math> reacts with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HF</mi></math> in presence of ammonia to give <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> which on thermal decomposition produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced></math> and ammonium fluoride. Oxidation state of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Be</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>BeO</mi></math> reacts with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HF</mi></math> in presence of ammonia to give <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> which on thermal decomposition produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced></math> and ammonium fluoride. Oxidation state of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Be</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> is
solution:
BeO  on reaction with HF in the presence of ammonia,  NH42BeF4 is formed which on heating forms BeF2  along with ammonium fluoride. 

BeO+2NH3+4HF→NH42BeF4+H2O

Compound A, is NH42BeF4

NH42BeF4→heatBeF2+2NH4F

Compound B is BeF2

In NH42BeF4 the oxidation state of Be is +2.

(I) CaOH2 (A) casts of statues
(II) NaCl (B) white wash
(III) CaSO4·12H2O (C) antacid
(IV) CaCO3 (D) washing soda preparation
solution:
(i) CaOH2 is used in white wash.

(ii) Plaster of paris is used in making of molds for plaster statues.

(iii) NaCl is used in preparation of washing soda.

(iv) A suspension of MgOH2 in water is used in medicine as an antacid under name of milk of magnesia.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List I with List II

List I List II
Elements Colour imparted to the flame
A K I Brick Red
B Ca II Violet
C Sr III Apple Green
D Ba IV Crimson Red

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

List I List II
Elements Colour imparted to the flame
A K I Brick Red
B Ca II Violet
C Sr III Apple Green
D Ba IV Crimson Red

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The colours of the flame are produced because of the movement of the electrons in the metal ions present in their salts. Calcium exhibits a brick-red colour. Strontium exhibits crimson colour. And the barium exhibits an apple green colour. 

K is an alkali metal. Potassium is colourless or white but when it is heated, it produces a violet flame due to the excitation of the electrons to the higher energy orbitals. Potassium burns with a light purple-coloured flame.

So, the correct option is C

q: MgNO32.XH2O and BaNO32.YH2O, represent formula of the crystalline forms of nitrate salts. Sum of X and Y is.......
question: MgNO32.XH2O and BaNO32.YH2O, represent formula of the crystalline forms of nitrate salts. Sum of X and Y is.......
solution:
Magnesium nitrate crystallises with six molecules of water, whereas barium nitrate is anhydrous salts. 

The hydrated form of magnesium nitrate is MgNO32.6H2O and anhydrous salt of barium nitrate is BaNO32 respectively
∴ x=6, y=0

question: Two elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have similar chemical properties. They don't form solid hydrogencarbonates, but react with nitrogen to form nitrides. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, respectively, are :
solution:
Li and Mg do not form solid bicarbonate. But react with N2 to give nitrides. Both of them have similar properties due to diagonal relationship.
6 Li+N2⟶Δ 2 Li3N
3 Mg+N2⟶ΔMg3N2

subject: Chemistry
q: An element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> shows similarity to an element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> belonging to group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has maximum hydration enthalpy in group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is
question: An element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> shows similarity to an element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> belonging to group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> has maximum hydration enthalpy in group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is
solution:
Hydration enthalpy is inversely related to the size of the ion.

Li+ has maximum hydration enthalpy in group 1 due to its small size.

Lithium shows diagonal relationship to magnesium.

The similarity between lithium and magnesium is particularly striking and arises because of their similar sizes : atomic radii, Li = 152 pm, Mg = 160 pm; ionic radii : Li+ = 76 pm, Mg2+= 72 pm.

The main points of similarity are

(I)Both lithium and magnesium are harder and lighter (ii) Lithium and magnesium react slowly with water. Their oxides and hydroxides are much less soluble and their hydroxides decompose on heating. Both form a nitride, Li3N and Mg3N2 , by direct combination with nitrogen. (iii) The oxides, Li2O and MgO do not combine with excess oxygen to give any superoxide. (iv) The carbonates of lithium and magnesium decompose easily on heating to form the oxides and CO2 .

Solid hydrogencarbonates are not formed by lithium and magnesium. (v) Both LiCl and MgCl2 are soluble in ethanol. (vi) Both LiCl and MgCl2 are deliquescent and crystallise from aqueous solution as hydrates, LiCl·2H2O and MgCl2 ·6H2O.

So 'B' is Mg.

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math>
solution:
Carbonates of alkaline earth metals are insoluble in water, and all the carbonates decompose on heating. Beryllium carbonate is unstable and can be kept only in the atmosphere of CO2. The thermal stability increases with increasing cationic size.
BeCO3<MgCO3<CaCO3<SrCO3<BaCO3
So the correct option is B.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): Gypsum is used for making fireproof wall boards.

Reason (R): Gypsum is unstable at high temperatures.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): Gypsum is used for making fireproof wall boards.

Reason (R): Gypsum is unstable at high temperatures.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct.
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct.
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
solution:
Gypsum board is also known as plasterboard, drywall or wall board. It consists of the paper surface and non-combustible core. These boards are easy to install. It has excellent fire resistance. Helps in sound isolation by preventing the transfer of unnecessary sound. It is less expensive and has great durability.

When exposed to high temperatures, gypsum only delays the temperature rise and cannot prevent shrinkage and crack from occurring, which can lead to the eventual collapse or failure of gypsum-based products.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

Its oxide <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MO</mi></math> is very stable to heat and does not have rock-salt structure. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is

subject: Chemistry
q: The magnetic behaviour of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Li</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively, are
question: The magnetic behaviour of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Li</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively, are
solution:
In the structure Li2O each lithium atom loses one electron to form 2 cations Li+ and oxygen atom gains two electrons to form the anion O2-.  It has no unpaired electrons. So, it is diamagnetic in nature. 

The peroxide O22- present in  Na2O2 has 4 electron in π*2p, thus all are paired. So, it is diamagnetic in nature.

In the structure KO2 oxygen is present in O2-. The number of electrons present is 17, where the where the sixteen electrons are from two oxygen atoms and one from the negative charge. There is one unpaired electron present in the last orbital. Hence, it is paramagnetic in nature. 

Li2O→ diamagnetic

Na2O2→ diamagnetic

KO2→ paramagnetic

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct order of hydration enthalpies is

(A) K+
(B) Rb+
(C) Mg2+
(D) Cs+
(E) Ca2+

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
The correct order of hydration enthalpies is

(A) K+
(B) Rb+
(C) Mg2+
(D) Cs+
(E) Ca2+

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The hydration enthalpy of alkali metal ions decreases with an increase in ionic sizes.

So, the order is: Li+ > Na+ >  K+ > Rb+ > Cs+

Like alkali metal ions, the hydration enthalpies of alkaline earth metal ions decrease with an increase in ionic size down the group.

So, the order is: Be2+ > Mg2+ > Ca2+ > Sr2+ > Ba2+ 

The hydration enthalpies of alkaline earth metal ions are larger than those of alkali metal ions. 

So, the correct order is: Mg2+ > Ca2+ > K+ > Rb+ > Cs+

(b) Lithium chloride is insoluble in pyridine.
(c) Lithium cannot form ethynide upon its reaction with ethyne.
(d) Both lithium and magnesium react slowly with H2O .
solution:
Lithium has highest hydration enthalpy among
alkali metals due to its small size.
LiCl is soluble in pyridine because LiCl have
more covalent character.
Li does not form ethynide with ethyne.
Both Li and Mg reacts slowly with H 2 O

Statement II : The magnesium ions are important for cell-membrane integrity.

In the light the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Lithium metal is used to make useful alloys, for example with lead to make ‘white metal’ bearings for motor engines, with aluminium to make aircraft parts, and with magnesium to make armour plates. So, Alloy of Li and Mg is used to make armour plates and not aircraft plates.

Calcium plays important roles in neuromuscular function, interneuronal transmission and cell membrane integrity

subject: Chemistry
q: Chlorides of which metal are soluble in organic solvents:
question: Chlorides of which metal are soluble in organic solvents:
solution:
As per Fajan's rule, Be cations has a higher charge and smaller size, as a result, have a higher charge density and covalent nature.

Ca, Mg, K are ionic compounds.

Hence, chlorides of Be will be more soluble in organic solvent.

q: The metal that forms nitride by reacting directly with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of air is:
question: The metal that forms nitride by reacting directly with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of air is:
solution:
Among the alkali metals, only lithium is able to form a stable nitride.

6Li+N2→2Li3N

                 Lithium nitride

q: On combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Li</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Na</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> in excess of air, the major oxides formed, respectively, are:
question: On combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Li</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Na</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> in excess of air, the major oxides formed, respectively, are:
solution: On heating in excess air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Li</mi></math> form oxide sodium form peroxide while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Rb</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cs</mi></math> form superoxide.
